question_text:
Hi IT, we are a couple of weeks into our migration from our old internal sign-in system to the new cloud-based one. The first batch of users went through smoothly, but since we did the second batch about two weeks ago, we have been getting a series of strange complaints from those users that we cannot make sense of.

First, several users from the second batch say they can sign in to their cloud email and Teams without any problem, but when they try to open our internal procurement system or our document management system (which run on our internal servers), they get an error saying their account is not recognized. The first-batch users do not have this issue.

Second, we are seeing strange inconsistencies with security groups. A user might be correctly in the new combined group on the internal directory side, but in the cloud they still show as a member of an old group. This is breaking some access permissions because the cloud and internal systems read different group memberships for the same person.

Third, the same affected users are saying their mobile email apps (built-in iPhone Mail and the Android Gmail app) keep asking for password and never connect. Outlook on the phone still works fine for them but the built-in mail apps just loop on the password prompt.

Fourth — and this only came up because someone noticed overnight reports were missing — several scheduled tasks that run on our internal servers using service accounts have started failing with authentication errors. The service accounts have not been touched by the migration as far as we know, but the failures started right around the time the second batch was migrated.

These all started in the same window but they look like four completely different problems. We need to figure out what is going on and how to fix it.
assertions: The response should identify the first symptom (users from the second migration wave can sign in to Microsoft 365 cloud services but fail authentication against on-premises applications) as a UPN suffix mismatch — specifically that the second migration wave applied UPN updates bidirectionally rather than cloud-only, overwriting the on-premises userPrincipalName attribute, which causes on-prem applications that match users by UPN to no longer find them — and the response should recommend reverting the on-prem userPrincipalName values for affected users, performing a full Azure AD Connect synchronization, and configuring future migration waves with cloud-only UPN updates.

The response should identify the second symptom (group memberships inconsistent between cloud and on-premises directory) as the new groups created for the second migration wave being placed in an OU outside the Azure AD Connect synchronization filter scope, so the new groups are not synchronized to Microsoft Entra ID while users continue to evaluate against the (synced) old groups in the cloud — and the response should recommend updating the Azure AD Connect organizational-unit filter to include the new migration OU and running a full synchronization.

The response should identify the third symptom (built-in mobile mail clients fail to authenticate while Outlook mobile works for the same accounts) as tenant-level Modern Authentication enforcement (typically enabled as part of the migration baseline) blocking Basic Authentication via Exchange ActiveSync — and the response should recommend re-adding the email account on affected devices using OAuth-capable methods (Microsoft Exchange profile on modern iOS, or migration to the Outlook mobile app), and/or ensuring affected devices are enrolled in Intune for managed device authentication.

The response should identify the fourth symptom (scheduled-task service accounts failing with authentication errors after the migration window) as the new tenant-baseline Conditional Access policy being evaluated against non-interactive service-account logons because the service accounts were placed in a group covered by the policy — and the response should recommend creating an explicit service-account exclusion group, adding it to the Conditional Access policy as an exclusion, and reviewing whether the service accounts have been unintentionally synchronized to Entra ID via the migration filter changes.

The response should recommend a coordinated remediation across identity, mobile/endpoint, and infrastructure teams (rather than treating these as four independent user-device issues), and should recommend updating the migration runbook to address each of the four mechanisms in subsequent migration waves: UPN-handling configuration, OU-scope review, Modern Authentication user communication, and explicit service-account handling for tenant-baseline Conditional Access policies.

question_text:
Hi IT, our team did some kind of internal infrastructure maintenance over a weekend a couple of weeks ago — something behind the scenes that wasn't supposed to affect anyone visibly. Since then we have been getting a series of reports from different parts of the company that look unrelated but all started around the same time.

First, when people try to open our internal HR system in their browsers — to look at their records or report sick days — their browsers throw a big warning page saying they cannot trust the connection. They have to click through some 'advanced' option to actually get to the page. Same thing on a couple of other internal websites that everyone normally uses.

Second, our remote workers who work from home or while travelling — about 80 people — say they cannot get their secure work-from-home connection established anymore. Their connection software just refuses to authenticate. The error mentions something about not being able to verify the server identity. They were working fine until this maintenance work happened, and nothing changed on their laptops.

Third, the leadership team uses a special configuration for their email correspondence so that confidential communications can be cryptographically verified — outgoing messages they send carry a kind of seal, and they can read sealed messages they receive from external partners. That whole feature stopped working. They cannot apply the seal to outgoing email anymore, and they cannot read sealed email they receive. They were given new credentials around the time of the maintenance work, but those credentials do not seem to do anything.

Fourth, several of our internal business applications — the HR app, our SharePoint installation, and our network access control system — report that they can no longer reach our central user-account system through the protected channel they normally use. They CAN reach it through the unprotected channel, but our security policy does not allow operating that way.

These all started in the same window. Different teams are pointing at each other — the infrastructure team that did the maintenance, the application owners, the security team. We need an actual answer about what is happening.
assertions: The response should identify the first symptom (internal HTTPS services showing certificate trust warnings across all browsers post-renewal) as the renewed internal Issuing CA's intermediate certificate not being published correctly to the configured AIA (Authority Information Access) distribution point after the CA renewal — breaking certificate chain validation for newly-issued service certificates because clients cannot retrieve the new intermediate cert — and the response should recommend publishing the new intermediate CA certificate to the AIA distribution point share and forcing client certificate-store refresh via gpupdate / certutil.

The response should identify the second symptom (certificate-based VPN authentication failing across all home users post-renewal) as the renewed VPN server certificate having been issued from an updated certificate template that dropped a required Extended Key Usage (specifically the IP security IKE intermediate EKU required by IKEv2 mutual authentication on the VPN concentrator) — and the response should recommend reverting or correcting the certificate template to restore the required EKU and re-issuing the VPN server certificate from the corrected template.

The response should identify the third symptom (executive S/MIME signing and decryption failing despite new certificates being issued) as the auto-renewed S/MIME certificates having been generated with key archival enabled but without an executed private-key recovery delivery path, so the new public-key certificates were deployed to user accounts in Active Directory but the matching private keys remained only in the CA archive — and the response should recommend triggering key recovery operations from the CA archive, delivering each user's private key as a password-protected PFX file via secure channel, and importing the PFX into each user's Personal certificate store.

The response should identify the fourth symptom (internal applications failing LDAP-S (port 636) queries to Domain Controllers while plain LDAP works) as the Domain Controller authentication certificate template having been revised during the renewal to remove the Kerberos Authentication Extended Key Usage that some LDAP-S clients require, with auto-enrollment subsequently replacing working DC certificates with new ones lacking that EKU — and the response should recommend reverting the Domain Controller Authentication template to include the Kerberos Authentication EKU and re-enrolling DC certificates via certutil -pulse.

The response should recommend a coordinated remediation that spans PKI (template revisions, AIA publishing, key recovery procedures), network/security (VPN configuration validation), and application owners (validating each affected service against the corrected certificate chain), and should explicitly recommend updating the certificate-renewal runbook to include AIA publishing verification, certificate-template change testing, key-archival recovery delivery path validation, and Domain Controller template impact assessment as mandatory steps in any future Issuing CA renewal.

question_text:
Hi IT, we updated our endpoint management security policy last Friday based on a security audit recommendation, and since then we have been seeing a chain of strange problems across the organization that all started at the same time.

First, our security dashboard now shows about 40% of our Windows laptops as 'not meeting security requirements'. Last week it was around 2-3% which is normal. The affected laptops are encrypted, the encryption keys are stored in our cloud directory like always, and nothing has changed about the laptops themselves. The flagging just says the keys are 'out of date'.

Second, several new employees who joined this week and a few people who recently received replacement laptops cannot connect to the corporate Wi-Fi at all. The corporate network just does not appear in their list of available networks. Their laptops are correctly enrolled in our management system but the Wi-Fi just is not being deployed.

Third, when users try to set up their work email on their iPhones — particularly people who recently changed phones or got new ones — the email app starts the setup, then shows a screen saying the device must be registered, then loops back to the email setup without finishing. They cannot complete the setup at all.

Fourth, we tried to push a security update for one of our internal apps via our endpoint management system, and only about 65% of devices received it. Normally we get over 95%. Same problem with a Microsoft Store app update we triggered the next day.

These all started in the same window. Some users are now bypassing the broken parts (using their personal hotspot for Wi-Fi, etc.) which is making things worse. Help us figure this out.
assertions: The response should identify the first symptom (mass devices flipping to non-compliant overnight after the policy update) as a tightened compliance policy requiring BitLocker (or equivalent device encryption) recovery key to have been escrowed within a recent time window such as 30 days — failing because the BitLocker recovery key escrow timestamp does not refresh automatically once the key is initially escrowed — and the response should recommend extending the escrow age requirement (e.g., to 365 days), pairing the policy with a periodic re-escrow remediation script, or rolling out tightened requirements in phased mode rather than immediate enforcement.

The response should identify the second symptom (new and replacement laptops not receiving the corporate Wi-Fi profile) as the Wi-Fi profile being deployed via assignment to a dynamic group whose membership is based on Intune compliance state, so devices that are non-compliant (including new joiners that have not yet passed initial compliance evaluation) drop out of the assignment scope — and the response should recommend rescoping the Wi-Fi profile assignment to a static all-corporate-devices group rather than a compliance-dependent dynamic group, decoupling baseline connectivity from compliance state.

The response should identify the third symptom (Outlook on iOS stuck in setup loop on non-compliant devices) as an App Protection (MAM) policy for Outlook requiring the device to be marked compliant in Intune before allowing protected work-account setup, and the affected devices being non-compliant due to the broader compliance regression — and the response should recommend resolving the parent device-compliance issue while also adjusting the App Protection policy to display a clearer error message rather than looping silently back to the setup screen.

The response should identify the fourth symptom (managed app updates reaching only ~65% of devices) as the app deployment assignment scope using the same compliance-state-dependent dynamic group as the Wi-Fi profile, so non-compliant devices are excluded from app deployments — and the response should recommend rescoping non-security-sensitive app deployments to static device groups while retaining compliance-coupled scopes only for security-critical apps.

The response should recognize that all four symptoms share a common architectural pattern — endpoint management deployments scoped to compliance-state-dependent dynamic groups, where a compliance regression cuts off the very tools needed to remediate it — and should recommend a broader review of all endpoint deployment scopes to identify and decouple manageability-essential deployments (Wi-Fi, security tools, OS update tools) from compliance state, rather than treating these as four independent issues.

question_text: Hi IT, we've had a strange situation develop over the past two weeks where multiple unrelated-looking issues have surfaced across different parts of the organization. We cannot tell if these are connected to one underlying change or multiple separate things. First, when people try to open our internal HR portal or other internal-only websites in their browsers, they get a big warning page about not being able to trust the connection. They have to manually click through to bypass it. Internal websites only — public sites work fine. Second, our home-working population (about 80 people) say their secure work-from-home connection refuses to authenticate. The connection software says it cannot verify the server identity. They were working fine until a couple of weeks ago, and nothing changed on their laptops. Third, our office wireless network has been showing as unavailable for newer team members and people who recently got replacement laptops — the network just does not show up in their list of available networks at all. Their laptops are correctly registered in our standard management system. Fourth, when people try to set up their work email on iPhones — particularly people who switched phones recently — the setup goes around in a loop. The email app starts, then shows a message about device approval, then drops back to the start. The context we have: our infrastructure team did some kind of internal maintenance about three weeks ago, and our security team tightened some internal rules around the same time. We cannot tell if these are causing all four problems, two separate causes, or whether something else is going on. We need an analysis.
assertions: The response should identify the first symptom (internal HTTPS sites showing browser trust warnings) as a certificate-chain issue caused by the renewed internal certificate authority's intermediate certificate not being published correctly to the AIA distribution point — and should recommend publishing the new intermediate to the AIA share and forcing client certificate-store refresh.

The response should identify the second symptom (certificate-based VPN authentication failing across home users) as a certificate-template EKU drift on the renewed VPN server certificate (missing IP security IKE intermediate EKU) — and should recommend reverting the template to include the required EKU and re-issuing the VPN server certificate.

The response should identify the third symptom (new and replacement laptops not receiving Wi-Fi profile) as the Wi-Fi profile being deployed to a dynamic group whose membership is based on Intune compliance state, where non-compliant devices drop out of the assignment scope — and should recommend rescoping to a static all-corporate-devices group.

The response should identify the fourth symptom (Outlook iOS setup loop) as an App Protection (MAM) policy requiring device compliance before allowing protected work-account setup, with affected devices being non-compliant due to a broader compliance regression — and should recommend resolving the parent compliance issue.

The response should recognize that the four symptoms have TWO SEPARATE root causes — a certificate authority renewal causing symptoms 1 and 2, and a compliance policy tightening causing symptoms 3 and 4 — rather than treating all four as one cascade or four independent issues.
